entities.name: PERMANGANATE OXIDIZABLE CARBON (POXC) METHOD
entities.description: A swift, dependable, and economical method used to assess the labile percentage of soil organic carbon and track variations in soil organic matter.

entities.name: SOIL ORGANIC CARBON (SOC)
entities.description: The carbon component of soil organic matter, specifically the labile percentage that is sensitive to environmental and managerial changes.

entities.name: MANAGERIAL ALTERATIONS
entities.description: Changes in land management practices that affect the labile percentage of soil organic carbon.

entities.name: ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
entities.description: External natural conditions that influence the labile percentage of soil organic carbon.

entities.name: ACTIVE ORGANIC MATTER
entities.description: The readily decomposable organic matter in the soil, denoted by POXC.

entities.name: SOIL QUALITY
entities.description: The capacity of a soil to function within an ecosystem, informed by the presence of active organic matter.

entities.name: CARBON CYCLES
entities.description: The process of carbon exchange between the atmosphere, biosphere, and soil.

entities.name: SOIL ORGANIC MATTER
entities.description: The organic component of soil, whose variations are tracked using the POXC method.

entities.name: SUSTAINABLE AGRICULTURE PRACTICES
entities.description: Agricultural methods designed to maintain soil health and productivity over the long term.

entities.name: SOIL HEALTH MANAGEMENT PROGRAMS
entities.description: Programs aimed at improving and maintaining the biological, chemical, and physical health of soil.

entities.name: ACTIVE CARBON METHOD
entities.description: The original method by Weil et al. (2003) upon which the POXC procedure is based.

entities.name: LAND INSTITUTE
entities.description: The institution where the Glover Lab is located, contributing adaptations to the POXC method.

entities.name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY
entities.description: The institution where the Barbercheck Lab is located, contributing adaptations to the POXC method.
